title: Regularization & Generalization
summary: Techniques to prevent overfitting. Explicit (dropout, weight decay, L1/L2) and implicit (SGD noise, data augmentation, early stopping). The goal: model learns patterns, not noise. Connects to pruning — L1 regularization creates prunable weights.
key_insights: Overfitting = model memorizes training data instead of learning patterns. Gap between train and val loss.
L1 drives weights to exactly zero (sparse). L2 drives weights to be small (not zero). L1 → natural pruning.
Dropout: randomly zero $p$ fraction of activations during training. Forces redundancy — no single neuron is critical.
Weight decay ≠ L2 regularization when using Adam. AdamW separates them: $\theta_{t+1} = (1-\lambda)\theta_t - \eta \cdot m_t/\sqrt{v_t}$.
SGD noise is FREE regularization — mini-batch gradients are noisy, preventing sharp minima. See BATCHING_SGD.
Early stopping: train until val loss stops improving. Simplest regularizer, often sufficient.

mathematical_core.l1: $$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{task}} + \lambda \sum_i |\theta_i|$$ Gradient: $\text{sign}(\theta_i)$ — constant push toward zero.
mathematical_core.l2: $$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{task}} + \lambda \sum_i \theta_i^2$$ Gradient: $2\lambda\theta_i$ — proportional push (larger weights shrink more).
mathematical_core.dropout: Training: $h' = h \odot m$ where $m \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$. Inference: $h' = (1-p) \cdot h$ (or inverted dropout: scale during training).

derivations.title: L1 vs L2 — why L1 creates sparsity

steps.name: L2 gradient near zero
steps.math: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\lambda\theta^2 = 2\lambda\theta$. As $\theta \to 0$: gradient $\to 0$. Shrinks slowly near zero, never reaches it.

steps.name: L1 gradient near zero
steps.math: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\lambda|\theta| = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta)$. Constant push of $\pm\lambda$ regardless of magnitude. Crosses zero and stays.

steps.name: Geometric view
steps.math: L1 constraint: diamond $\sum|\theta_i| \leq c$. L2 constraint: sphere $\sum\theta_i^2 \leq c$. Diamond has corners on axes — intersections with loss contours hit corners = sparse solutions.

derivations.consequence: L1 produces sparse models naturally. L2 produces small-but-dense models. For pruning: train with L1, then remove zero weights.

derivations.title: Dropout as ensemble approximation

steps.name: Each forward pass = different sub-network
steps.math: With $n$ neurons and dropout $p$: $2^n$ possible sub-networks. Each training step trains a random one.

steps.name: Inference = geometric average of all sub-networks
steps.math: Scaling by $(1-p)$ at inference approximates the geometric mean of all $2^n$ sub-network predictions.

derivations.consequence: Dropout ≈ training an exponential ensemble. Each member sees different features → forces the network to learn redundant representations.

geometric_properties: L1 constraint region = diamond (axis-aligned corners encourage sparsity)
L2 constraint region = sphere (rotationally symmetric, no axis preference)
Dropout creates a different loss surface each step — smooths the optimization landscape
statistical_properties: Train-val gap > 5-10% relative: likely overfitting. Add regularization or more data.
Dropout $p$: typical values 0.1 (transformers) to 0.5 (FC layers). Higher $p$ = stronger regularization.
Weight decay $\lambda$: typical $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$. Too high = underfitting.
numerical_properties: Dropout is FREE at inference if using inverted dropout (scale during training).
L1 is not differentiable at $\theta = 0$. Use subgradient: any value in $[-1, 1]$.
Weight decay on bias terms: generally not done (bias doesn't cause overfitting).

related: FOUNDATIONS.BATCHING_SGD — SGD noise is implicit regularization
